content.full_text:
RAy! MEHTA
CHAIRMAN
FAIR POLJTICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
July 14,
1995
Richard K. Denhalter
City Attorney
City of San Juan Capistrano
32400 Paseo Adelanto
San Juan Capistrano, California
92675
Re:
Your Request for Informal
Assistance
Our File No.
1—95—135
Dear Mr.
Denhalter:
As the city attorney for the City of San Juan Capistrano
you are requesting advice regarding the mass mailing provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the “Act”) •1
Since your request does
not involve a specific pending decision,
we are treating it as one
for informal assistance, pursuant to Regulation 18329(c).
2
QUESTIONS
1.
Under the current mass mailing regulations,
are cities
allowed to publish newsletters containing the names and titles of
the current elected officials?
2.
Is a publication exempt from the mass mailing regulations
if it is- paid for by public funds but not funds of the, public
agency whose elected officials are featured in the publication?
1
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Conuujssion
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000—18954.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Sec
tion 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).).
428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


File No.
Page
2
CONCLUSIONS
1.
A city may publish a newsletter containing the flames and
titles of elected officials provided the names and titles appear
only as permitted by Regulation 18901.
2.
If an elected officer who is featured in a mass mailing
is not affiliated with the agency which produces or sends the mass
mailing, the mailing is not prohibited by subdivision
(a)
of
Regulation 18901 and, therefore,
does not violate the prohibition
of Section 89001.
FACTS
You have submitted a copy of a newsletter published by
another local agency; the newsletter includes a roster listing of
all city councilmembers,
including their names and titles,
and
also includes a description of city council highlights,
again
including their names and titles.
You have asked whether a
similar newsletter to be published by your agency is permissible
•under the mass mailing provisions of the Act.
You have submitted another publication which includes the
names and photographs of elected officials of a certain county.
Although the publisher of the publication sold advertising space
to,
and thus received funds from, the cities located in the
county,
no county funds were used for the publication.
You have
asked whether the inclusion of the names and photographs of county
elected officials in a similar publication to be publIshed by your
agency is permissible under the mass mailing provisions of the
Act.
ANALYSIS
-- 1.
Are cities allowed to publish newsletters containing the
names and titles of the- current elected officials?
-
-
Except in certain circumstances,
Section 89001,
as
interpreted and implemented by Regulation 18901, prohibits elected
officials and their agencies from sending a mass mailing
3 at
government expense which either:
-
-
(A)
Features an elected officer affiliated
with the agency which produces or sends the mail
ing,
or
Section 82041.5 and Regulation 18901(a) (4) essentially define
“mass mailing” for purposes of Section 89001 as over 200
unsolicited and substantially similar items sent in a calendar
month.


rue
flO.
A—1Jb
Page
3
(B)
Includes the name, office, photograph,
or
other reference to an elected officer affiliated
with the agency which produces or sends the mail
ing,
and is prepared or sent in cooperation,
consultation, coordination, or concert with the
elected officer.
Regulation 18901(a) (2) (A)
and
(B).
Subdivisions
(c) (1)
and
(c) (2)
of Regulation 18901 define the
terms “elected officer affiliated with the agency” and “features
an elected officer” as follows:
(1)
“Elected officer affiliated with the
agency” means an elected officer who is a member,
officer, or employee of the agency,
or of a subunit
thereof such as a committee, or who has supervisory
control over the agency, or who appoints one or
more members of the agency.
(2)
“Features an elected officer” means that
the item mailed includes the elected officer’s
photograph or signature,
or singles out the elected
officer by the manner of display of his or her name
or office in the layout of the document,
such as by
headlines, captions, type size, typeface,
or type
color.
However, subdivision
(b) (1)
of Regulation 18901 exempts from
the prohibition on the above described mass mailings:
Any item in which the elected officer’s name
appears only in the letterhead or logotype of the
—stationery,
forms
(including “For Your Information”
or “Compliments o” cards),
and envelqpes of the
agency sending the mailing, or of a committee of
the agency, or of the elected officer,
or in a
roster listing containing the names of all elected
officers of the agency.
In any such item, the
names of all elected officers must appear in-the
same type size, typeface,- type color,
and location.
Such item may not include the elected officer’s
photograph, signature,
or any other reference to
the elected officer,
except as specifically
permitted in this subdivision
(b) (1)
or elsewhere
in this regulation.
In addition, subdivision
(b) (8)
of Regulation 18901 exempts
from the mass mailing prohibition the sending of a mass mailing
containing:
A telephone directory, organization chart,
or
similar listing or roster which includes the names
of elected officers as well as other individuals in


File No.
Page 4
the agency sending the mailing,
where the name of
each elected officer and individual listed appears
in the same type size, typeface,
and type color.
Such item may not include an elected officer’s
photograph,
name, signature, or any other reference
to an elected officer,
except as specifically
permitted in this subdivision
(b) (8)
or elsewhere
in this regulation.
Thus,
a city may:
o
publish a newsletter containing the name and title of an
elected official provided the elected official is not
featured,
and the publication is not prepared in
coàperation, consultation,
coordination or concert with
the elected official.
(Regulation 189.01(a) (2) (A)
and
(B).)
o
publish a newsletter containing the names and titles of
elected officials in a roster listing provided the names
and titles appear only as permitted by subdivisions
(b) (1)
and
(b) (8)
of Regulation 18901,
as described
above.
In addition, other provisions of Regulation 18901 provide
specific exemptions which permit the use of an elected official’s
name in a mass mailing.
2.
Is a publication exempt from the mass mailing regulations
if it is paid for by public funds but not funds of the public
agency whose elected officials are featured in the publication?
As noted above,
Section 89001,
as interpreted and implemented
by Regulation 18901, prohibits elected-officials and their
—
agenc.ies from sending a mas ma-iliiig at government expense which
either:
-
-
(A)
Features an elected officer affiliated
with the agency which produces or sends the mail
ing
1 or
-
-
(B)
Includes the name, office, photograph,
or
other reference to an elected officer affiliated
with the agency which produces or sends the mail
ing,
and is prepared or sent in cooperation,
consultation, coordination,
or concert with the
elected officer.
Regulation 18901(a) (2) (A)
and
(B).
Thus, if a mass mailing includes the name of an elected
official,
or if the elected official is featured in the mass
mailing,
and if the elected official is not affiliated with the
agency which produces or sends the mass mailing, the mailing is


Page
5
not prohibited by subdivision
(a)
of Regulation 18901 and,
therefore, •does not violate the prohibition of Section 89001.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please
contact me at 916/322-5901
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Jeevan Ahuja
Counsel,
Legal Division
SGC:JA:ak
